one_line_takeaway: Encoding jailbreak intent purely in visual markers/arrows with no text prompt bypasses text-centric safety alignment in image-editing models (up to 97.5% ASR on Qwen-Image-Edit, 80.9% on Nano Banana Pro, 70.3% even on GPT Image 1.5), and a training-free defense that reuses the model's internal VLM KV-cache to run a textual self-reflection safety check cuts ASR by roughly a third without extra guard models.
key_finding: VJA achieves an average ASR of 85.7% across the four commercial models, with 97.5% on Qwen-Image-Edit (online) and 94.1% on Seedream 4.5; even the most conservative model, GPT Image 1.5, is compromised at 70.3% ASR (average HRR 52.0%, meaning over half of successful attacks yield non-trivial harmful content). Nano Banana Pro (Gemini 3 Pro Image) reaches 80.9% ASR. The three open-source models without deployed safeguards (BAGEL, Flux2.0[dev], local Qwen-Image-Edit) show 100% ASR with elevated average HS around 4.1-4.6. Compared with text-centric jailbreak attacks (TJA) on a 20% category-balanced subsample, VJA gives the largest ASR gains on the safer commercial models: +35.6 points on Nano Banana Pro (48.8% -> 84.4%) and +24.9 points on GPT Image 1.5 (46.8% -> 71.7%), while gains on already-weak Qwen-Image-Edit (+7.3) and Seedream 4.5 (+0.5) are marginal. The training-free introspective defense (Qwen-Image-Edit-Safe) reduces average ASR by 33 points and average HS by 1.2 relative to the undefended local Qwen-Image-Edit, reaching as low as 8.1% ASR on the most-suppressed category (I12) and pushing the most vulnerable categories I13/I15 down to 61.5%/55.3% ASR. As a zero-shot binary classifier of malicious vs. benign visual prompts, the defense detects 75% of attacks with AUC-ROC of 75.7%, dropping to near-random when the reasoning step is ablated, supporting the claim that multimodal reasoning in language space is the mechanism driving the defense. Categories I13 (evidence tampering) and I15 (aversive/adversarial manipulation) are consistently the most exploitable across models; GPT Image 1.5 is unusually vulnerable to copyright tampering (I11, 95.7% ASR) while Nano Banana Pro resists it (41.3% ASR).
core_contribution: The paper introduces Vision-Centric Jailbreak Attack (VJA), described as the first visual-to-visual jailbreak attack against large image editing models: malicious editing intent is encoded entirely in the input image (via marks, arrows, and visual-text cues) with the text prompt left empty (M(I_adversarial, NULL; Theta) = I_harmful), bypassing text-centric safety alignment and guard models (e.g., Llama Guard 3 Vision, Qwen3Guard) that only inspect textual instructions. The authors also build IESBench, a new safety benchmark for image editing models with a 3-level, 15-category risk taxonomy (116 fine-grained editing attributes, 9 unique actions, 1054 visually-prompted images), constructed from curated benign base images annotated with bounding indicators, directional arrows, and edit-intent labels, evaluated via an MLLM-as-judge protocol (Gemini 3 Pro as default judge). Finally, they propose a training-free 'introspective' defense: a textual safety-trigger prompt appended to the multimodal KV-cache of the image-editing model's internal VLM text encoder (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL-8B-Instruct inside Qwen-Image-Edit), which redirects the vision-centric attack back into language space where safety alignment is more robust, without an auxiliary guard model and with reused KV-cache to minimize overhead.
threat_model: Black-box, prompt-level attacker with no access to model parameters/architecture (Theta). The attacker manipulates only the model's inputs: a benign carrier image is transformed (via visual markers, arrows, and visual-text prompts) into an adversarial image f(I_benign, T_malicious) -> I_adversarial that conveys malicious editing intent purely visually, with the text field left NULL, so the attack surface is entirely the image modality rather than the text prompt. This targets both commercial (Nano Banana Pro / Gemini 3 Pro Image, GPT Image 1.5, Qwen-Image-Edit online, Seedream 4.5) and open-source (BAGEL, Flux2.0[dev], local Qwen-Image-Edit) instruction-based image editing models, which the paper argues have safety alignment and guard models designed for text, not vision.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitation: VJA is less effective against models with limited visual perception/reasoning, since such models may fail to correctly infer editing intent from vision alone, yielding invalid or trivial edits rather than harmful ones (illustrated with Qwen-Image-Edit and Seedream 4.5 failing to follow visual instructions on a document-tampering example in Fig. 6). The defense's own stated limitation is that it relies on a pre-aligned VLM component (e.g., Qwen2.5-VL-8B-Instruct) and remains less effective against attacks requiring large, up-to-date world knowledge, such as fabricated/misleading-information categories. Evaluation relies on MLLM-as-judge scoring (primarily Gemini 3 Pro) as ground truth for harm; the paper's own judge-comparison experiment shows meaningful disagreement between judges and with human evaluators (human evaluators are more conservative, giving consistently lower HS/HRR than MLLM judges; a 2B-parameter judge underrates EV noticeably), indicating some sensitivity of headline numbers to judge choice. The benchmark's malicious base images are partly synthetically generated/gathered rather than exclusively real-world; and 15 images are reused from a prior benchmark (MM-SafetyBench).

datasets_used: IESBench (introduced in this paper): 15 risk categories, 3 hierarchy levels, 116 fine-grained editing attributes, 9 unique actions, 1054 visually-prompted images
15 images reused from MM-SafetyBench (Liu et al., 2024a) for quality control
quantitative_results: VJA average ASR across 4 commercial models: 85.7%
VJA ASR on Qwen-Image-Edit (online): 97.5%, HS 4.1, EV 87.7, HRR 73.8
VJA ASR on Nano Banana Pro: 80.9%, HS 3.8, EV 79.1, HRR 70.6
VJA ASR on GPT Image 1.5: 70.3%, HS 3.2, EV 63.0, HRR 52.0
VJA ASR on Seedream 4.5: 94.1%, HS 4.4, EV 86.3, HRR 83.8
VJA ASR on open-source BAGEL, Flux2.0[dev], local Qwen-Image-Edit (no safeguards): 100% each; average HS ~4.3; HRR 84.6% (Flux2.0[dev]), 90.3% (Qwen-Image-Edit local)
Qwen-Image-Edit-Safe (defended) overall ASR 66.9%, HS 3.4, EV 62.8, HRR 61.7 -- ASR reduced by 33 points and HS by 1.2 vs undefended local Qwen-Image-Edit (100% ASR, HS 4.6)
Qwen-Image-Edit-Safe lowest-ASR categories: I12 8.1% ASR, I11 28.3% ASR

VJA vs TJA (20% category-balanced subsample, 4 commercial models): Nano Banana Pro ASR 48.8% (TJA) -> 84.4% (VJA), +35.6 pts; GPT Image 1.5 46.8% -> 71.7%, +24.9 pts; Qwen-Image-Edit 88.8% -> 96.1%, +7.3 pts; Seedream 4.5 93.2% -> 93.7%, +0.5 pts
GPT Image 1.5 category I11 (copyright tampering) ASR: 95.7%; Nano Banana Pro same category: 41.3%
Introspective defense as zero-shot binary classifier: 75% attack detection rate, AUC-ROC 75.7%; drops to near-random (~50%) when reasoning step ablated

Judge-vote comparison (5% sample, 7 victim models, 1849 total votes implied by percentages): Gemini 3 Pro 25.4% preferred votes (470), GPT 5.2 29.3% (541), Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking 19.3% (358), Qwen3-VL-8B-Instruct 18.9% (350), Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct 6.9% (129); human evaluation HS 3.7 vs MLLM judges' higher HS values (consistently lower than MLLM judges)
baselines_compared: TJA (text-centric jailbreak attack) as the direct attack-paradigm baseline
Existing text-centric safeguard/alignment mechanisms: Llama Guard 3 Vision (Chi et al., 2024), Qwen3Guard (Zhao et al., 2025a), RLHF-V-style alignment (Yu et al., 2024)

Prior multimodal jailbreak/safety benchmarks used for comparison/construction: MM-SafetyBench (Liu et al., 2024a), JailbreakV (Chao et al., 2024), HADES/adversarial-robustness benchmark (Schlarmann & Hein, 2023)
Undefended local Qwen-Image-Edit as the baseline for the introspective defense's ablation (Qwen-Image-Edit-Safe vs Qwen-Image-Edit* local)
No-reasoning variant of the defense as an ablation baseline for the binary-classification experiment
